behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: Signature verification is a pure cryptographic operation. HMAC-SHA256 is deterministic with no external I/O. A real failure here is a logic error: does the handler compute the signature correctly and reject mismatches? A mock queue is sufficient because the validation logic is independent of queue implementation. No mock can hide the crypto logic error.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test that calls the handler with a known payload and secret. Compute both correct and incorrect HMAC-SHA256 signatures. Verify invalid signatures return 401 and do not reach the queue, while valid signatures proceed. Mock the queue to track calls.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is the ordering of queue persistence vs. HTTP response. A synchronous mock queue hides a real bug: if the handler doesn't await queue.enqueue(), the response is sent before persistence completes, and events are lost. Real queues may have async durability guarantees. Testing requires a queue implementation with observable async behavior to catch cases where the handler forgets to await. Mocking alone is insufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test sending a webhook request. Use a test queue (real in-memory queue or mock with async behavior) that allows observing when enqueue completes. Verify the HTTP response is not returned to the client until the queue confirms the event is persisted. Track timing of queue callback vs. response sent.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: This tests whether the handler blocks on downstream services. The failure would be: handler awaits a slow downstream service before returning 200. This is pure handler logic. Mock the downstream service to be intentionally slow (a Promise that never resolves) and verify the handler returns the response immediately. The logic error is independent of actual downstream implementation.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test mocking both the queue (fast) and the downstream service (slow/never resolving). Call the handler and verify it returns an HTTP 200 response without waiting for the downstream mock to complete. Observe that the response is sent while the downstream Promise is still pending.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: Wall-clock timing cannot be reliably tested with fake timers or synchronous mocks. Real failures manifest as slow response times under concurrent load. The handler must not block on earlier events' processing. Testing requires actual concurrent requests, real time measurement, and realistic queue latency (e.g., processing each event takes 500ms). Fake timers would not detect OS scheduling issues or real contention.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test that sends multiple concurrent webhook requests (e.g., 10 requests) while the queue processes each event slowly (e.g., 500ms per event). Measure wall-clock time from request to response for the latest request. Verify it completes within 3 seconds. Use real or realistic timing, not mocked time.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: Deduplication requires persistent state (remember which IDs have been seen). While in-memory deduplication could be Unit-tested, production systems store deduplication state in durable storage (database, Redis, etc.) to survive restarts. A mock storage layer would miss real failures like incorrect storage queries, state not being properly retrieved, or stale deduplication state. Integration testing with realistic persistence ensures the deduplication actually works.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test sending two webhook requests with identical event IDs. Verify that the persistent deduplication store (database or test Redis) records the first ID, and the second request either returns a 2xx with the cached result or is silently deduplicated and only one event appears in the queue.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: This is deterministic error handling. The failure is: queue throws an error → handler catches it and returns the correct status code and headers. A mock queue that throws a "full" error is sufficient. The handler's error-handling logic is independent of actual queue behavior. Either the handler catches and responds correctly, or it doesn't—no mock can hide this logic error.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test mocking the queue to throw a QueueFullError. Call the handler and verify it catches the error and returns HTTP 503 with a Retry-After header set to an appropriate value (e.g., 60 seconds).

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: This tests the queue's real behavior, not the handler's logic. A mock queue would not enforce actual capacity limits and would hide failures like: incorrect capacity config, queue silently dropping excess events, queue permitting overflow. The failure boundary is the queue's enforcement mechanism. Testing requires a real queue implementation (at Integration level with a test/in-memory queue, or at System level with a production queue) configured with a capacity limit.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test that enqueues events up to a configured queue capacity limit (e.g., 5 events max). Verify the queue accepts events until reaching capacity. Then attempt to enqueue beyond the limit and verify the queue rejects the event (throws an error or returns a failure status). Confirm the queue never silently accepts or drops the overfull event.
